resolution_shaft_seven_close.speaker: brooks
resolution_shaft_seven_close.line_1: Brooks reports the extraction status and the remaining shaft activity. The false report is in evidence custody, and one signatory remains on site.
resolution_shaft_seven_close.line_2: Saye records the Institute authentication protocol. The signatory has to speak on record while the shaft is still listening.

buttons.label: Force the repudiation
buttons.preview: Get a signed, witnessed repudiation of the false report on record, satisfying the closure condition

buttons.label: Torah speaks to the shaft
buttons.preview: Use Torah's symbolic authority to force the shaft's closure condition through direct witness rather than institutional...

operation_plans.label: Force the repudiation
operation_plans.primary_skill: evidence confrontation and institutional record procedure
operation_plans.tactical_step: Symbolic close: Brooks presents the timestamped report to the remaining signatory, walks them through the authentication protocol on comms with Saye as witness, and files the formal repudiation before the contractor vehicle is out of range
operation_plans.intent: Get a signed, witnessed repudiation of the false report on record, satisfying the closure condition
operation_plans.yield: Shaft pressure ceases; the replicating document stops propagating; Reiss's extraction holds; the Institute has a clean record of what happened
operation_plans.risk: The signatory refuses or recants; the repudiation is incomplete; the shaft interprets a partial admission as continued concealment and the pressure pulses resume

lines: Brooks sets the file on the break room table and does not sit down. He reads the timestamp aloud. He does not editorialize. The signatory looks at the table for a long time. Saye's voice comes through the comms, steady: 'I'm recording. Whenever you're ready.' What follows is not dramatic. It is a person saying, in plain language, what they did and why. The shaft goes quiet before they finish. The concrete stops cracking. Brooks logs the timestamp of the repudiation and does not look at the signatory again.

The signatory admits to the timestamp but not to intent. Brooks accepts it. Saye logs it. The shaft pressure drops but does not stop entirely. One pulse, low, every few minutes, like a reminder. The replicating document slows but does not clear from all terminals. Brooks files what he has. It is enough to close the site. It is not enough to close the case.

The signatory says the timestamp was a clerical error. Brooks does not argue. He logs the denial, packs the file, and walks out. Behind him, the shaft emits one long, low pulse that cracks the break room window from the inside. Saye says, quietly on comms: 'The document is still propagating.' Brooks does not respond. He already knows.

operation_plans.label: Torah speaks to the shaft
operation_plans.tactical_step: Symbolic close: Torah addresses the shaft directly, naming the sin aloud in operational terms, standing at the lip as witness, which satisfies the judgment-engine's requirement for acknowledgment even without a full institutional repudiation
operation_plans.intent: Use Torah's symbolic authority to force the shaft's closure condition through direct witness rather than institutional process
operation_plans.yield: Shaft closes immediately; Reiss stabilizes; the false report stops propagating; but the institutional record remains incomplete, which creates a future pressure vector
operation_plans.risk: The shaft interprets Torah's address as an attempt to substitute authority for accountability; the pressure pulses redirect toward Torah personally

lines: Torah walks to the shaft lip and looks down. The gantry is gone or groaning. Below, Reiss looks up. Torah does not raise his voice. He names what happened here: a person was put below because others could not bear that he was chosen. He names it as a fact, not a sermon. The shaft is silent for three seconds. Then the pressure stops. The concrete stops cracking. Reiss, from below or from the edge if already extracted, says nothing. He does not need to. The break room light goes out on its own.

Torah speaks and the shaft responds, but not cleanly. The pressure drops by half. The gantry stabilizes. Reiss can be extracted now, or holds his position more easily. But the false report is still on every terminal in the building, and the signatory in the break room has not moved. Torah has bought time. The shaft is not closed. It is waiting.

Torah speaks and the shaft answers. The pressure pulse that comes back is not random. It is directed. It hits the gantry frame directly above Torah's position and the vibration travels through the concrete into his feet and up. Owen grabs his arm. 'Step back.' Torah steps back. The shaft is still open. Below, Reiss's voice rises again, steady and patient, and the concrete cracks another centimeter outward.

Brooks sets the file on the break room table and does not sit down. He reads the timestamp aloud. He does not editorialize. The signatory looks at the table for a long time. Saye's voice comes through the comms, steady: 'I'm recording. Whenever you're ready.' What follows is not dramatic. It is a person saying, in plain language, what they did and why. The shaft goes quiet before they finish. The concrete stops cracking. Brooks logs the timestamp of the repudiation and does not look at the signatory again.

Torah walks to the shaft lip and looks down. The gantry is gone or groaning. Below, Reiss looks up. Torah does not raise his voice. He names what happened here: a person was put below because others could not bear that he was chosen. He names it as a fact, not a sermon. The shaft is silent for three seconds. Then the pressure stops. The concrete stops cracking. Reiss, from below or from the edge if already extracted, says nothing. He does not need to. The break room light goes out on its own.

cooldown_shaft_seven_debrief.speaker: brooks
cooldown_shaft_seven_debrief.line_1: 0520. The site is cordoned. Reiss is in the clinic van. Brooks is sitting on the hood of the Institute vehicle with the evidence sleeve in his lap.
cooldown_shaft_seven_debrief.line_2: Owen is at the water station, drinking slowly. She has not spoken since the extraction. Saye is still on comms, waiting for someone to close the channel.

lines: Brooks has not put the evidence sleeve down. He holds it the way people hold things they are not sure what to do with.

Owen finishes the water and crushes the cup. She looks at the shaft housing, which is now just a concrete collar in the ground. 'He was down there for eleven hours,' she says. Not to anyone. Just to say it.
Saye's voice comes through the comms, quieter now: 'Torah. I need to know if the repudiation was filed or if we're managing this another way. I need to know before I write the clinic intake.'
The contractor flatbed is gone or it is still at the gate. Either way, the site feels smaller than it did at 0340.

visible_text: Brooks has not put the evidence sleeve down. He holds it the way people hold things they are not sure what to do with.

Owen finishes the water and crushes the cup. She looks at the shaft housing, which is now just a concrete collar in the ground. 'He was down there for eleven hours,' she says. Not to anyone. Just to say it.
Saye's voice comes through the comms, quieter now: 'Torah. I need to know if the repudiation was filed or if we're managing this another way. I need to know before I write the clinic intake.'
The contractor flatbed is gone or it is still at the gate. Either way, the site feels smaller than it did at 0340.
choices: Tell Saye the record is clean
Tell Saye the record is incomplete
Say nothing and let Brooks answer.
outcomes: If the repudiation was filed: Saye logs it, closes the channel professionally, and does not ask follow-up questions. Brooks finally puts the sleeve in the case file. Owen looks at Torah once, briefly, and nods.

If the record is incomplete: Saye is quiet for a moment, then says 'Understood' in a tone that means she will carry this. Brooks looks at the ground. Owen does not react visibly, which is its own reaction.

If Torah says nothing: Brooks answers for him. Whatever Brooks says, it is accurate. He does not soften it. Saye logs it. The channel closes. Brooks looks at Torah and does not say what he is thinking.
followup_hooks: The two colleagues who left in the contractor vehicle are now a loose thread in the Institute record
The false report's replication pattern, if not fully cleared, may surface in another site's document system
Reiss, once stable, will need to decide whether to file a formal complaint or accept the Institute's handling; his decision depends on what Torah told Saye
Owen's rope-rescue performance under structural failure has been noted; someone at the Institute will ask about it

resolution_forty_day_window.speaker: SITREP
resolution_forty_day_window.line_1: The survivor is stabilized, and the echo is suppressed but not closed. The HR archive terminal is open on the supervisor's desk with the grievance file on screen.
resolution_forty_day_window.line_2: Saye reports that the file documents both the original assault and the retaliatory scope. The echo's interval is holding, but not indefinitely.

buttons.label: Torah: read the file aloud
buttons.preview: Close the echo by naming the excess; accept the weight of speaking someone else's unconfessed accountability

buttons.label: Destroy the cabinet
buttons.preview: Reduce the echo's lethality by destroying its physical anchor; create a window for Torah to work...

operation_plans.label: Torah: read the file aloud
operation_plans.tactical_step: symbolic close: Torah reads the grievance file aloud on-site, names the original wrong, then explicitly names the retaliatory scope as disproportionate; the echo requires both named, in order
operation_plans.intent: Close the echo by naming the excess; accept the weight of speaking someone else's unconfessed accountability
operation_plans.yield: Echo extinguishes; the repeating sound stops; the lacerations on surviving casualties stop deepening; the mission closes with a remainder: the third party who countersigned the closure is named in the file
operation_plans.risk: If Torah names the grievance but hesitates on the excess, the echo intensifies for one final pass before Torah can finish; partial naming is worse than silence

lines: Torah reads the file from the beginning. The original assault. The names. The dates. The retaliatory attack, the four workers who had nothing to do with it, the scope of what the brothers did. Then Torah says it plainly, without editorial: the retaliation was disproportionate. The excess was real. The grievance did not justify the scope. The repeating impact sound slows on the second sentence and stops before the third. The overhead lights do not change. The floor is very quiet. Saye, over comms, after a moment: 'Casualty lacerations are stable. They're not deepening.' Ross is looking at Torah. Owen is looking at the floor. Brooks, from the ground level: 'Is it done?' Torah says yes. There is a name at the bottom of the file, a countersignature on the closure. Torah photographs it before closing the terminal.

Torah reads the file and names the original wrong clearly. Then Torah reaches the retaliatory scope and the words slow. The excess is there in the document but saying it feels like indicting someone who had a reason. Torah says it, but the phrasing hedges: understandable given the circumstances. The echo does not stop. It makes one more pass, fast, and Owen takes contact on her arm before Torah says it again, without the hedge. The sound stops. Owen is pressing her sleeve against the cut. She does not look at Torah. The file is still open. The countersignature is visible at the bottom.

Torah reads the grievance and stops at the retaliatory scope. The words are there. Torah does not say them. The echo completes a full pass through the squad's position. Ross takes contact. Owen takes contact. Torah closes the terminal. The sound does not stop. Saye, over comms, her voice stripped of everything except information: 'It's not closing. Whatever you need to say, you need to say it now.' Torah looks at the screen. The terminal is closed. The file is gone from view. The echo is still moving.

operation_plans.label: Destroy the cabinet
operation_plans.tactical_step: breach and destroy: Ross forces or breaks the supervisor's cabinet, removes and destroys the cutting tools used in the original retaliation; this suppresses the echo's range and speed but does not close it; buys time for the symbolic close
operation_plans.intent: Reduce the echo's lethality by destroying its physical anchor; create a window for Torah to work the archive without contact pressure
operation_plans.yield: Echo's movement range contracts to a five-meter radius around the cabinet's former position; the interval lengthens; squad has a clear window to reach the archive terminal
operation_plans.risk: Destroying the tools without the symbolic close leaves the echo suppressed but active; it will re-anchor to the next available object connected to the original act within the facility

lines: Ross hits the cabinet lock with the butt of his weapon twice and it gives. The tools inside are industrial, heavy, the kind that leave the marks Saye described. He pulls them out and breaks them against the press housing, one by one. The repeating impact sound does not stop but it contracts, pulling back toward the cabinet's position like something on a leash. The center aisle is clear. Torah has a window. Ross, breathing hard, to Torah: 'You've got maybe four minutes before it finds something else to anchor to. The file. Now.'

Ross gets the cabinet open but the tools are heavier than expected and the destruction takes longer. He gets two of the three broken before the echo reorients and finds him at the cabinet. The contact is brief, a cut across his knuckles, and he finishes the third tool with his hand bleeding. The echo contracts but not as far as it should. One tool is still intact inside the cabinet, wedged in the back. Ross cannot reach it without his hand inside the echo's contracted range. He steps back. 'Torah. It's suppressed but not contained. The file is your window.'

Ross forces the cabinet and the tools are there but the echo does not respond to the destruction the way the pattern suggested it would. It does not contract. It intensifies. The sound gets louder and the interval drops to three seconds. Ross steps back from the cabinet. The tools are broken on the floor and it does not matter. Saye, over comms: 'Ross, the physical anchor isn't the primary anchor. It's the file. The tools were secondary. Get Torah to the terminal.' Ross relays this without commentary. His voice is flat.

Torah reads the file from the beginning. The original assault. The names. The dates. The retaliatory attack, the four workers who had nothing to do with it, the scope of what the brothers did. Then Torah says it plainly, without editorial: the retaliation was disproportionate. The excess was real. The grievance did not justify the scope. The repeating impact sound slows on the second sentence and stops before the third. The overhead lights do not change. The floor is very quiet. Saye, over comms, after a moment: 'Casualty lacerations are stable. They're not deepening.' Ross is looking at Torah. Owen is looking at the floor. Brooks, from the ground level: 'Is it done?' Torah says yes. There is a name at the bottom of the file, a countersignature on the closure. Torah photographs it before closing the terminal.

Ross hits the cabinet lock with the butt of his weapon twice and it gives. The tools inside are industrial, heavy, the kind that leave the marks Saye described. He pulls them out and breaks them against the press housing, one by one. The repeating impact sound does not stop but it contracts, pulling back toward the cabinet's position like something on a leash. The center aisle is clear. Torah has a window. Ross, breathing hard, to Torah: 'You've got maybe four minutes before it finds something else to anchor to. The file. Now.'

cooldown_forty_day_window.speaker: brooks
cooldown_forty_day_window.line_1: Brooks finds Torah in the stairwell after the floor is cleared. He does not ask for a debrief. He asks what closed it.
cooldown_forty_day_window.line_2: When Torah tells him, Brooks is quiet for a long time. Then: 'Someone had to say it. Shouldn't have been you.'

lines: Owen is sitting on the bottom stair with her jacket off, pressing a field dressing to her arm. She is not looking at anyone. Ross is leaning against the wall across from her, his own dressing already taped. He asks Saye, still on comms, whether the brothers knew what they were starting. Saye says: 'I think they knew exactly what they were starting. I think that was the point.' Ross nods once and does not ask a follow-up. Owen says nothing. She has been saying nothing since the floor went quiet.

Brooks walks the perimeter of Floor 3 before he comes down. When he does, he has the facility supervisor's key ring in his hand, which he sets on the stair railing without explanation. He looks at Torah. 'The name at the bottom of the file. The countersignature. You get it?' Torah confirms. Brooks: 'That person is at the Harlan secondary site. Forty minutes from here.' He does not say what should be done about it. He does not have to.

The survivor, a floor worker named Dara, is in the ground-floor break room with two paramedics. She has not asked what happened. She has asked, twice, whether the men who were hurt are going to be all right. Nobody has answered her yet.

visible_text: Owen is sitting on the bottom stair with her jacket off, pressing a field dressing to her arm. She is not looking at anyone. Ross is leaning against the wall across from her, his own dressing already taped. He asks Saye, still on comms, whether the brothers knew what they were starting. Saye says: 'I think they knew exactly what they were starting. I think that was the point.' Ross nods once and does not ask a follow-up. Owen says nothing. She has been saying nothing since the floor went quiet.

Brooks walks the perimeter of Floor 3 before he comes down. When he does, he has the facility supervisor's key ring in his hand, which he sets on the stair railing without explanation. He looks at Torah. 'The name at the bottom of the file. The countersignature. You get it?' Torah confirms. Brooks: 'That person is at the Harlan secondary site. Forty minutes from here.' He does not say what should be done about it. He does not have to.

The survivor, a floor worker named Dara, is in the ground-floor break room with two paramedics. She has not asked what happened. She has asked, twice, whether the men who were hurt are going to be all right. Nobody has answered her yet.
choices: Tell Owen she held long enough
outcomes: If Torah acknowledges Owen: she looks up, once, and then back at her hands. She says 'I know' in a tone that means she is not sure she believes it yet. Her fixation on the cabinet does not resolve tonight.
If Torah acknowledges Ross: he straightens slightly. 'Saye kept me honest on the measurements. Credit her.' He means it. The squad's cohesion holds.

If Torah flags the countersignature for Institute review: Brooks picks up the key ring and pockets it. 'Then we wait.' His tone makes clear he does not think waiting is the right answer. The follow-up pressure is live.
If Torah decides to pursue the countersignature directly: Brooks nods. 'Secondary site. I'll pull the layout.' Owen, from the stairs, without looking up: 'Give me an hour.'
followup_hooks: The countersignature belongs to a mid-level administrator at the Harlan secondary site who closed the grievance file without reading it; the wrath-echo's remainder is anchored to that unread closure
Owen's fixation on the cabinet and her silence in the cooldown are early indicators of symbolic contamination; if she deploys to the secondary site without a clinic check, her mental state degrades
Ross's laceration, if untreated, carries a low-grade symbolic contamination marker that Saye flagged but did not report formally; she will bring it up if Ross is assigned to the follow-up

corpus_anchor_points.followup_payoff: If the squad breaks contact without the symbolic close, the echo follows the Institute's scent; it becomes a follow-up threat

resolution_enough_until_morning.speaker: dr_samira_iyad
resolution_enough_until_morning.line_1: Iyad reports the clinic canteen stable but unresolved. The food remains in their hands, and extraction alone will not close it.
resolution_enough_until_morning.line_2: Torah reports the queue outside still waiting. The sign-in sheet lists one hundred and nine names, and the surplus is warm.

buttons.label: Distribute to the queue
buttons.preview: Torah takes the corrected log outside, addresses the queue, and organizes the redistribution of the surplus—every...

buttons.label: Destroy the surplus, log it
buttons.preview: If the queue is gone or redistribution is impossible, Owen organizes the witnessed destruction of the...

operation_plans.label: Distribute to the queue
operation_plans.tactical_step: symbolic close—Torah leads the redistribution of the bay discharge and remaining cold-store contents to the waiting queue under witnessed record; Owen corrects and co-signs the distribution log
operation_plans.intent: Torah takes the corrected log outside, addresses the queue, and organizes the redistribution of the surplus—every unit logged against a name, witnessed by Owen and Brooks, until the store is empty
operation_plans.yield: Full closure: seated patients recover as the surplus moves outward; log corrected and signed; anomaly resolves completely; no remainder in the building
operation_plans.risk: If the queue is gone, or if the redistribution is incomplete, the closure is partial; if Torah is not present at the moment of distribution, the symbolic weight does not transfer

lines: Torah carries the corrected log to the loading bay door and opens it. The queue outside is still there—sixty, maybe seventy people, some of them sitting on the curb. Torah does not make a speech. Owen reads names from the sign-in sheet and people come forward and receive what was logged for them. It takes forty minutes. The bay floor is clean by the time the last name is called. Inside, Iyad reports that the seated group has stopped chewing. They are sitting with their hands in their laps, looking at the tables. One of them asks, quietly, if there is any water. There is.

The redistribution runs until the queue thins, but not everyone on the sign-in sheet is still outside—maybe forty names go unclaimed. Owen logs them as undelivered and notes the time. The seated group reduces from fourteen to nine. The remaining nine are still chewing when the bay is empty. Iyad says, 'It is not enough. The log still has a gap.' Owen looks at the unclaimed names. 'We can deliver. If we know where they are.' Torah says, 'Find out.' It is not a full close. It is a direction.

The queue has dispersed—Brooks did not hold them, and without information they left. The bay is full of surplus with nowhere to go. Torah stands in the loading bay with the corrected log and no recipients. Owen says, 'We can call the secondary distribution sites. It will take hours.' The seated group inside is now sixteen. Iyad's voice on the radio is careful and even: 'I need a decision. The longer this sits, the more people sit with it.'

operation_plans.label: Destroy the surplus, log it
operation_plans.primary_skill: evidence destruction under witness, accountability record
operation_plans.tactical_step: symbolic close via destruction—Owen directs the controlled destruction of the surplus under witnessed record as an alternative closure if redistribution fails; Torah co-signs the destruction log
operation_plans.intent: If the queue is gone or redistribution is impossible, Owen organizes the witnessed destruction of the surplus—documented, signed, and logged against the original inventory—as the secondary closure condition
operation_plans.yield: Partial closure: anomaly resolves but with a remainder; seated patients recover more slowly; the log is corrected but the gap is noted as unresolved
operation_plans.risk: Destruction without full witness chain or without Torah's co-signature does not satisfy the symbolic condition; the remainder persists and may reactivate at a secondary site

lines: Owen runs the destruction by the book. Every crate logged, every unit counted, the forklift used to move it to the incinerator bay two blocks over under Institute transport authority. Torah co-signs each page. It takes three hours. The seated group recovers in stages—first they stop lifting the food, then they put it down, then they look around as if they have just woken up in an unfamiliar room. One woman says she is very tired. Iyad tells her that is normal and means it. The log has a note on the final page in Owen's handwriting: 'Surplus destroyed in lieu of distribution. Recipients unlocated. Case open for follow-up delivery.' She underlines the last four words.

The destruction is witnessed but Torah is called away before the final pages are signed—Brooks needs a command decision on the perimeter. Owen signs alone. The seated group recovers to eleven. Three remain at the tables, hands in laps, not eating, not responding. Iyad sits with them. She does not try to move them. She says, 'I think they need the delivery to happen. The record is not enough for them.' Owen looks at the three remaining patients and then at the unsigned pages. 'I'll find Torah.'

The destruction log is incomplete—Voss took the master inventory and the remaining pages do not account for the full surplus. Owen cannot certify what was destroyed because she cannot certify what was there. She submits the partial record to Institute command with a cover note. The seated group recovers to eight. Six remain. Iyad does not leave the canteen. When Torah checks on her an hour later, she is sitting at one of the tables—not eating, not chewing, just sitting—and she looks up and says, 'I am not one of them. I just did not want them to be alone.' Torah believes her. Mostly.

Torah carries the corrected log to the loading bay door and opens it. The queue outside is still there—sixty, maybe seventy people, some of them sitting on the curb. Torah does not make a speech. Owen reads names from the sign-in sheet and people come forward and receive what was logged for them. It takes forty minutes. The bay floor is clean by the time the last name is called. Inside, Iyad reports that the seated group has stopped chewing. They are sitting with their hands in their laps, looking at the tables. One of them asks, quietly, if there is any water. There is.

Owen runs the destruction by the book. Every crate logged, every unit counted, the forklift used to move it to the incinerator bay two blocks over under Institute transport authority. Torah co-signs each page. It takes three hours. The seated group recovers in stages—first they stop lifting the food, then they put it down, then they look around as if they have just woken up in an unfamiliar room. One woman says she is very tired. Iyad tells her that is normal and means it. The log has a note on the final page in Owen's handwriting: 'Surplus destroyed in lieu of distribution. Recipients unlocated. Case open for follow-up delivery.' She underlines the last four words.

cooldown_enough_until_morning.speaker: dr_samira_iyad
cooldown_enough_until_morning.line_1: Iyad is in the loading bay, sitting on a crate that has been emptied and turned upside down. She has her gloves off. She is looking at her hands.
cooldown_enough_until_morning.line_2: Ross is leaning against the forklift. He has not mentioned his arm again. Owen is on the phone, reading names from the sign-in sheet to someone at a secondary site.

lines: Iyad says, without looking up: 'The ones who recovered—they all said the same thing. That they were not hungry. That they had never been hungry. That they just could not stop.'
Ross says: 'That tracks.'
Owen ends her call. 'Fourteen names located. Delivery confirmed for tomorrow morning.' She pauses. 'Six still unaccounted for.' She writes something in the log margin. 'I will find them.'
Iyad finally looks up. 'The food in their hands never went down. Not one gram. I weighed a piece before and after.' She puts her gloves in her kit. 'I do not know what to do with that.'
Ross: 'Write it up. That is what you do with it.'
Iyad: 'Yes.' She does not sound convinced. She sounds like someone who will write it up and then think about it for a long time afterward.

visible_text: Iyad says, without looking up: 'The ones who recovered—they all said the same thing. That they were not hungry. That they had never been hungry. That they just could not stop.'
Ross says: 'That tracks.'
Owen ends her call. 'Fourteen names located. Delivery confirmed for tomorrow morning.' She pauses. 'Six still unaccounted for.' She writes something in the log margin. 'I will find them.'
Iyad finally looks up. 'The food in their hands never went down. Not one gram. I weighed a piece before and after.' She puts her gloves in her kit. 'I do not know what to do with that.'
Ross: 'Write it up. That is what you do with it.'
Iyad: 'Yes.' She does not sound convinced. She sounds like someone who will write it up and then think about it for a long time afterward.
outcomes: If Owen's follow-up is authorized: the six names are located within forty-eight hours; the log is closed; the case is marked resolved with remainder noted. Owen's evidence chain is intact.
If Ross goes to clinic: Iyad has a patient to focus on and does not sit with the question of the food weight until she is ready. Her stress does not compound tonight.
If the squad delays: Iyad is found in the canteen at 0300 by Brooks, who was doing a perimeter check. She is sitting at the same table. She has not eaten anything. She is fine. She says she is fine.
followup_hooks: The six unlocated recipients from the sign-in sheet—if Owen does not find them, they appear in a later mission as a secondary anomaly site
Voss is not in custody; his location is unknown; the torn inventory pages are a future evidence hook
Ross's arm—the discharge contact was brief, but Iyad noted it; if his swallow reflex shows any irregularity in the next seventy-two hours, she has a protocol for it and she will use it
The corrected distribution log, if fully signed and filed, becomes an Institute accountability record that reduces scrutiny pressure in a future base_fallout event

resolution_kaph_file_and_evacuate.speaker: dr_lenora_saye
resolution_kaph_file_and_evacuate.line_1: Saye reports the incident form open. She needs the report read aloud, word for word, while the door reaches increment four.
resolution_kaph_file_and_evacuate.line_2: Ross reports the damaged file readable. Enough evidence survives to file, and the door will not release until the account is rendered.

buttons.label: File the report with Saye
buttons.preview: Ross renders the account that Holt buried—reads every legible entry to Saye, who formally logs it...

buttons.label: Force the door, evacuate now
buttons.preview: Brooks bets that physical force can hold the door long enough to get everyone out, accepting...

operation_plans.label: File the report with Saye
operation_plans.primary_skill: evidence custody and formal reporting under duress
operation_plans.tactical_step: Symbolic close: Ross reads the recovered report aloud to Saye over the relay; Saye logs it into the Institute's reporting chain; the door cycle releases
operation_plans.intent: Ross renders the account that Holt buried—reads every legible entry to Saye, who formally logs it, satisfying the closure condition and releasing the door
operation_plans.yield: Door cycle halts and begins slow reversal; Vasek evacuates; station is accessible; Holt's negligence is on record
operation_plans.risk: The water-damaged entries create gaps in the record; partial filing releases the door but leaves the anomaly incompletely logged; remainder persists

lines: Ross reads. His voice is steady—steadier than it has any right to be. Saye repeats each entry back before logging it, her voice flat and procedural, the way you talk when you are keeping yourself out of it. The door stops mid-grind. The grinding does not resume. Vasek walks out through the gap under his own power. Owen watches the door. It does not move. 'Log complete,' Saye says. 'Incident filed. Eleven weeks, partial record.' A pause. 'It was enough.'

Ross reads what he can. Three entries are illegible—water damage, Holt's handwriting bleeding into the margin. Saye logs what she receives. The door slows but does not stop. It advances one final increment and holds there—gap still passable, barely. Vasek squeezes through. Ross follows. The door does not seal. It does not open further. Park looks at the gap. 'It's waiting,' she says. 'For the rest of the record.' The rest of the record does not exist.

The relay drops before Ross finishes the third entry. Saye's voice cuts to silence. Ross keeps reading into dead air—he reads the whole report to no one, standing at Holt's duty desk, because there is nothing else to do. The door advances to increment five. The gap closes. The grinding stops. Owen is outside. She puts her hand flat against the sealed door and holds it there for a moment. Then she steps back. 'Mark it,' she says. 'Sealed. Personnel inside. Unresolved.'

operation_plans.label: Force the door, evacuate now
operation_plans.primary_skill: breaching and forced extraction under time pressure
operation_plans.tactical_step: Breach and evacuate: Brooks drives the breaching tool into the door track to arrest the final increment, holds the gap by force, and extracts all personnel before attempting any filing
operation_plans.intent: Brooks bets that physical force can hold the door long enough to get everyone out, accepting that symbolic closure may be incomplete or delayed
operation_plans.yield: All personnel evacuated; door held open; report in hand for later filing; station accessible but anomaly not fully resolved
operation_plans.risk: Door resumes cycling after eight minutes; breaching tool fails or is destroyed; Brooks is injured; door seals with the tool inside and the gap lost

lines: Brooks hits the door track with the breaching tool and the grinding stops—not released, arrested. The gap holds. 'Eight minutes,' he says. 'Move.' Ross and Vasek move. Owen counts them through. Brooks holds the tool in place with both hands, and the door pushes back against him the entire time—not mechanical resistance, something else, something that knows it is being delayed. Everyone is out in four minutes. Brooks pulls the tool at six. The door resumes and seals at seven. The report is in Ross's hands. Saye is still on the relay. 'Read it to me,' she says.

The breaching tool catches the track and holds for six minutes before the door mechanism finds a way around it—not mechanical, it simply resumes from a different point in the cycle. Brooks pulls the tool before it is destroyed. The gap is narrower now. Ross gets through. Vasek gets through. Brooks gets through last, and the door clips his shoulder on the way—not a crush, a graze, but he feels it. He does not mention it until the debrief. The report is out. The door is sealed. The station is closed but not permanently. 'We have the report,' Owen says. 'That's what we came for.'

The breaching tool goes into the track and the door takes it. The mechanism does not stop—it incorporates the tool, grinds through it, and advances. Brooks watches the tool disappear into the door frame. The gap closes to thirty centimeters. 'Pull back,' Owen says. Brooks does not move immediately. 'Brooks.' He pulls back. The door seals. The grinding stops. The ant-pattern interference on Park's terminal resolves into a single repeating output: the same sensor entry from week three, the one Holt buried first. Park screenshots it. It is the only piece of the record they have.

Ross reads. His voice is steady—steadier than it has any right to be. Saye repeats each entry back before logging it, her voice flat and procedural, the way you talk when you are keeping yourself out of it. The door stops mid-grind. The grinding does not resume. Vasek walks out through the gap under his own power. Owen watches the door. It does not move. 'Log complete,' Saye says. 'Incident filed. Eleven weeks, partial record.' A pause. 'It was enough.'

Brooks hits the door track with the breaching tool and the grinding stops—not released, arrested. The gap holds. 'Eight minutes,' he says. 'Move.' Ross and Vasek move. Owen counts them through. Brooks holds the tool in place with both hands, and the door pushes back against him the entire time—not mechanical resistance, something else, something that knows it is being delayed. Everyone is out in four minutes. Brooks pulls the tool at six. The door resumes and seals at seven. The report is in Ross's hands. Saye is still on the relay. 'Read it to me,' she says.

cooldown_kaph_debrief.speaker: lt_mara_owen
cooldown_kaph_debrief.line_1: Owen sets the rubber-banded log stack on the debrief table. She does not open it. She says Holt's name once and does not say it again.
cooldown_kaph_debrief.line_2: Brooks has his jacket off. The shoulder graze is visible. He is talking about something that happened three years ago, unrelated, and no one interrupts him.

lines: Owen: 'Two months ago I flagged his logs. They told me the paperwork was in order.'
Ross sets the water-stained report on the table next to Owen's log stack. He does not say anything about it.
Park is still at her terminal. She has not closed the screenshot of the week-three sensor entry. She is not looking at it. She is not looking away from it either.
Brooks: 'You know what I keep thinking about? That chair. Facing away from the door. Who sits like that at a watch station?'

Saye's voice on the relay, closing out: 'Partial record accepted. Incident logged. Holt, Dren—status unknown. Station Kaph—accessible, anomaly remainder flagged for follow-up.' A pause. 'Good work. All of you.' She closes the line before anyone can respond.
environment_state_changes: character.lt_mara_owen.morale: vindicated but unresolved—Holt is still missing
character.brooks.injury: minor, logged, not treated yet
institute.reporting_chain.status: partial record filed, remainder flagged

visible_text: Owen: 'Two months ago I flagged his logs. They told me the paperwork was in order.'
Ross sets the water-stained report on the table next to Owen's log stack. He does not say anything about it.
Park is still at her terminal. She has not closed the screenshot of the week-three sensor entry. She is not looking at it. She is not looking away from it either.
Brooks: 'You know what I keep thinking about? That chair. Facing away from the door. Who sits like that at a watch station?'

Saye's voice on the relay, closing out: 'Partial record accepted. Incident logged. Holt, Dren—status unknown. Station Kaph—accessible, anomaly remainder flagged for follow-up.' A pause. 'Good work. All of you.' She closes the line before anyone can respond.
outcomes: If Ross is safe: the squad is intact but the partial record sits on the table between them like an open question. Owen's vindication is real and tastes like nothing.
If Ross is sealed inside: the debrief does not happen in the usual way. Owen writes the incident report herself, longhand, and files it before anyone else is awake.
If Brooks was injured: he accepts clinic referral only after Owen stops asking and Park starts. He goes because Park asked, not because Owen ordered it.
followup_hooks: Holt's location is unresolved—he left the station before the door began cycling, which means he knew
The week-three sensor entry that the interference kept repeating is the only complete record of what the anomaly actually was—Park has the screenshot
The partial record creates an Institute audit flag: someone will come to close the remaining eleven weeks, and they will find gaps that cannot be filled
Ross read the full report aloud to dead air—he knows everything in it, including the entries that were not transmitted to Saye

resolution_borrowed_separation_contact_c.line_1: SITREP reports Reiss separated from the column by force, wig evidence cut, and Column C-4 holding under temporary shoring.
resolution_borrowed_separation_contact_c.line_2: Brooks is injured but standing outside the breach point. The sign is broken rather than ceremonially closed, and Saye and Torah disagree about what finishes the record.

buttons.label: Saye: Full Disclosure
buttons.preview: Saye comes off remote comms and enters the site to take custody of the evidence personally...

buttons.label: Torah: Release the Vow
buttons.preview: Torah speaks the release directly—not as a religious performance but as the symbolic authority the anomaly...

operation_plans.label: Saye: Full Disclosure
operation_plans.primary_skill: anomaly pattern documentation, symbolic evidence analysis
operation_plans.tactical_step: evidence custody of wig remains and Separation Athletics materials, formal anomaly report with manufacturer identification, CASEVAC handoff to Institute medical with full disclosure of anomaly mechanism
operation_plans.intent: Saye comes off remote comms and enters the site to take custody of the evidence personally. She documents the wig remains, the protocol guide, and the manufacturer's materials as a complete anomaly package. She gives Reiss a full account of what happened to him—not a cover story. She files the manufacturer's identity with the Institute for follow-up action.
operation_plans.yield: Complete evidence chain. Reiss understands what was done to him. The Institute has the manufacturer's identity. The anomaly is documented in a way that prevents recurrence. Saye's silence during the mission is explained by what she found in the data—she was building the disclosure package.
operation_plans.risk: Full disclosure means the Institute's involvement is on record. The manufacturer has resources. The department head who denied the wig remains request is now on notice. Saye's report creates institutional pressure that may not resolve in the squad's favor.

lines: Saye is in Bay 3 within eight minutes of the all-clear. She does not look at the column. She looks at the bench—the packaging, the guide, the crushed water bottle. She photographs everything before she touches anything. When she sits down next to Reiss, she does not use clinical language. She tells him what the product was, what it did, and why it is not his fault. Reiss listens. He does not say anything for a long time. Then he asks who made it. Saye tells him. She has already filed the name. On the drive back, she tells Torah what she found in the thermal data that made her go quiet—a second heat signature, consistent with a second unit of the product, sold to a second buyer, location unknown. She says it the way you say something you have been holding for two hours.

Saye takes custody of the evidence. The disclosure to Reiss is partial—she tells him enough to explain the physical symptoms, not enough to explain the full anomaly mechanism. She is not lying; she is managing what he can absorb right now. The manufacturer's identity goes into the Institute file. The department head's denial of the wig remains request is noted but not escalated. The second heat signature is in Saye's personal notes, not the official report. She will decide later whether to include it. That decision will follow her.

Saye arrives on site and the evidence is already compromised—the protocol guide is missing from the bench, removed by a civilian first responder who thought it was personal property before Ross could stop them. The wig remains are in the evidence container but the chain of custody has a gap. Saye files what she has. The manufacturer's identity is in the report but the physical evidence is incomplete. Reiss is in Institute medical and has not been told anything. Saye sits in the vehicle for a long time after filing. Ross finds her there an hour later. He does not ask what she is thinking. He already knows.

operation_plans.label: Torah: Release the Vow
operation_plans.primary_skill: symbolic authority, vow acknowledgment, anomaly closure
operation_plans.tactical_step: symbolic close—Torah formally acknowledges the false vow's end in the presence of the wig remains, establishing that the separation is completed and the obligation discharged, followed by CASEVAC handoff and building clearance
operation_plans.intent: Torah speaks the release directly—not as a religious performance but as the symbolic authority the anomaly requires to close. The false vow was imposed without consent; Torah's acknowledgment that the separation is ended, in the presence of the remains, is the condition the anomaly's logic requires. This is the closure condition that force cannot provide.
operation_plans.yield: The anomaly closes completely. Any residual strength in the wig remains dissipates. Reiss's body begins to recover. The column fracture stops propagating. The squad has a clean close. Torah carries the weight of having spoken into something they do not fully understand.
operation_plans.risk: If Torah speaks the release and the anomaly does not close—if the wig was not sufficiently destroyed, or if the burn was incomplete—the discharge pulse reads Torah's authority as a challenge rather than a completion. The residual force redirects. The squad is inside the building when it does.

lines: Torah stands at the column base with the evidence container open in front of them. The wig remains are inside—cut, partially burned, still warm. Owen is at the breach point. Brooks is outside. Park is watching the column. Torah does not perform anything. They say, plainly, that the separation is ended, that the obligation is discharged, that what was taken without consent is returned. The warmth in the container goes out. The column does not make another sound. Reiss, on the stretcher at the east entrance, stops shaking. Ross reports from the perimeter that the instrument readings have dropped to baseline. Saye, on comms, exhales. It is the first sound she has made in twenty minutes. Owen does not say anything. She marks the building clear and starts the CASEVAC handoff. She will not talk about this part of the debrief.

Torah speaks the release. The anomaly responds—the warmth in the container drops, the column fracture stops—but the close is incomplete. A section of the wig's base is still adhered to Reiss's scalp, in Institute medical custody now, and the obligation has not fully discharged. Reiss's tremor continues at a lower level. The instrument readings drop but do not reach baseline. The anomaly is suppressed. It is not closed. Saye flags the residual in her report. The evidence container goes into restricted storage. Torah knows, without being told, that this will come back.

Torah speaks the release and the container gets hot. Not warm—hot, the kind of heat that makes Park step back and Owen call everyone to the breach point. The discharge pulse moves through the floor. The temporary column props hold but one of them buckles. The building alarm triggers. Everyone exits. The container is left inside—too hot to carry. Ross seals the east entrance. The instrument readings spike and then drop to a level that Saye, on comms, describes as 'not baseline.' She does not elaborate. The building is condemned. The container is inside it. Torah stands at the cordon line and does not speak for the rest of the operation. Owen files the after-action report. She leaves Torah's role in the resolution section blank and sends it to Saye to complete.

Saye is in Bay 3 within eight minutes of the all-clear. She does not look at the column. She looks at the bench—the packaging, the guide, the crushed water bottle. She photographs everything before she touches anything. When she sits down next to Reiss, she does not use clinical language. She tells him what the product was, what it did, and why it is not his fault. Reiss listens. He does not say anything for a long time. Then he asks who made it. Saye tells him. She has already filed the name. On the drive back, she tells Torah what she found in the thermal data that made her go quiet—a second heat signature, consistent with a second unit of the product, sold to a second buyer, location unknown. She says it the way you say something you have been holding for two hours.

Torah stands at the column base with the evidence container open in front of them. The wig remains are inside—cut, partially burned, still warm. Owen is at the breach point. Brooks is outside. Park is watching the column. Torah does not perform anything. They say, plainly, that the separation is ended, that the obligation is discharged, that what was taken without consent is returned. The warmth in the container goes out. The column does not make another sound. Reiss, on the stretcher at the east entrance, stops shaking. Ross reports from the perimeter that the instrument readings have dropped to baseline. Saye, on comms, exhales. It is the first sound she has made in twenty minutes. Owen does not say anything. She marks the building clear and starts the CASEVAC handoff. She will not talk about this part of the debrief.

operation_plans.label: Saye: Full Disclosure
operation_plans.primary_skill: anomaly pattern documentation, symbolic evidence analysis
operation_plans.tactical_step: evidence custody of wig remains and Separation Athletics materials, formal anomaly report with manufacturer identification, CASEVAC handoff to Institute medical with full disclosure of anomaly mechanism
operation_plans.intent: Saye comes off remote comms and enters the site to take custody of the evidence personally. She documents the wig remains, the protocol guide, and the manufacturer's materials as a complete anomaly package. She gives Reiss a full account of what happened to him—not a cover story. She files the manufacturer's identity with the Institute for follow-up action.
operation_plans.yield: Complete evidence chain. Reiss understands what was done to him. The Institute has the manufacturer's identity. The anomaly is documented in a way that prevents recurrence. Saye's silence during the mission is explained by what she found in the data—she was building the disclosure package.
operation_plans.risk: Full disclosure means the Institute's involvement is on record. The manufacturer has resources. The department head who denied the wig remains request is now on notice. Saye's report creates institutional pressure that may not resolve in the squad's favor.

lines: Saye is in Bay 3 within eight minutes of the all-clear. She does not look at the column. She looks at the bench—the packaging, the guide, the crushed water bottle. She photographs everything before she touches anything. When she sits down next to Reiss, she does not use clinical language. She tells him what the product was, what it did, and why it is not his fault. Reiss listens. He does not say anything for a long time. Then he asks who made it. Saye tells him. She has already filed the name. On the drive back, she tells Torah what she found in the thermal data that made her go quiet—a second heat signature, consistent with a second unit of the product, sold to a second buyer, location unknown. She says it the way you say something you have been holding for two hours.

Saye takes custody of the evidence. The disclosure to Reiss is partial—she tells him enough to explain the physical symptoms, not enough to explain the full anomaly mechanism. She is not lying; she is managing what he can absorb right now. The manufacturer's identity goes into the Institute file. The department head's denial of the wig remains request is noted but not escalated. The second heat signature is in Saye's personal notes, not the official report. She will decide later whether to include it. That decision will follow her.

Saye arrives on site and the evidence is already compromised—the protocol guide is missing from the bench, removed by a civilian first responder who thought it was personal property before Ross could stop them. The wig remains are in the evidence container but the chain of custody has a gap. Saye files what she has. The manufacturer's identity is in the report but the physical evidence is incomplete. Reiss is in Institute medical and has not been told anything. Saye sits in the vehicle for a long time after filing. Ross finds her there an hour later. He does not ask what she is thinking. He already knows.

operation_plans.label: Torah: Release the Vow
operation_plans.primary_skill: symbolic authority, vow acknowledgment, anomaly closure
operation_plans.tactical_step: symbolic close—Torah formally acknowledges the false vow's end in the presence of the wig remains, establishing that the separation is completed and the obligation discharged, followed by CASEVAC handoff and building clearance
operation_plans.intent: Torah speaks the release directly—not as a religious performance but as the symbolic authority the anomaly requires to close. The false vow was imposed without consent; Torah's acknowledgment that the separation is ended, in the presence of the remains, is the condition the anomaly's logic requires. This is the closure condition that force cannot provide.
operation_plans.yield: The anomaly closes completely. Any residual strength in the wig remains dissipates. Reiss's body begins to recover. The column fracture stops propagating. The squad has a clean close. Torah carries the weight of having spoken into something they do not fully understand.
operation_plans.risk: If Torah speaks the release and the anomaly does not close—if the wig was not sufficiently destroyed, or if the burn was incomplete—the discharge pulse reads Torah's authority as a challenge rather than a completion. The residual force redirects. The squad is inside the building when it does.

lines: Torah stands at the column base with the evidence container open in front of them. The wig remains are inside—cut, partially burned, still warm. Owen is at the breach point. Brooks is outside. Park is watching the column. Torah does not perform anything. They say, plainly, that the separation is ended, that the obligation is discharged, that what was taken without consent is returned. The warmth in the container goes out. The column does not make another sound. Reiss, on the stretcher at the east entrance, stops shaking. Ross reports from the perimeter that the instrument readings have dropped to baseline. Saye, on comms, exhales. It is the first sound she has made in twenty minutes. Owen does not say anything. She marks the building clear and starts the CASEVAC handoff. She will not talk about this part of the debrief.

Torah speaks the release. The anomaly responds—the warmth in the container drops, the column fracture stops—but the close is incomplete. A section of the wig's base is still adhered to Reiss's scalp, in Institute medical custody now, and the obligation has not fully discharged. Reiss's tremor continues at a lower level. The instrument readings drop but do not reach baseline. The anomaly is suppressed. It is not closed. Saye flags the residual in her report. The evidence container goes into restricted storage. Torah knows, without being told, that this will come back.

Torah speaks the release and the container gets hot. Not warm—hot, the kind of heat that makes Park step back and Owen call everyone to the breach point. The discharge pulse moves through the floor. The temporary column props hold but one of them buckles. The building alarm triggers. Everyone exits. The container is left inside—too hot to carry. Ross seals the east entrance. The instrument readings spike and then drop to a level that Saye, on comms, describes as 'not baseline.' She does not elaborate. The building is condemned. The container is inside it. Torah stands at the cordon line and does not speak for the rest of the operation. Owen files the after-action report. She leaves Torah's role in the resolution section blank and sends it to Saye to complete.

She has already filed the name. On the drive back, she tells Torah what she found in the thermal data that made her go quiet—a second heat signature, consistent with a second unit of the product, sold to a second buyer, location unknown. She says it the way you say something you have been holding for two hours.

The warmth in the container goes out. The column does not make another sound. Reiss, on the stretcher at the east entrance, stops shaking. Ross reports from the perimeter that the instrument readings have dropped to baseline. Saye, on comms, exhales. It is the first sound she has made in twenty minutes. Owen does not say anything. resolution_borrowed_separation_active.speaker: SITREP
resolution_borrowed_separation_active.line_1: SITREP reports the first intervention failed. Reiss remains active at Column C-4, and the building is inside the collapse window.
resolution_borrowed_separation_active.line_2: The squad has no clean closure option from inside Bay 3. The next decision is containment under failure conditions: hold the site remotely or pull the cordon before the structure decides for them.

buttons.label: Saye: Remote Containment
buttons.preview: Saye holds the site remotely while Ross seals the outer cordon.

buttons.label: Owen: Pull Cordon
buttons.preview: Owen pulls everyone outside and preserves personnel before evidence.

operation_plans.label: Saye: Remote Containment
operation_plans.primary_skill: anomaly pattern documentation, symbolic evidence analysis
operation_plans.tactical_step: remote containment: keep Reiss and the column under instrument lock, seal the east entrance, and hold the squad outside the collapse radius while Saye builds a second-entry protocol
operation_plans.intent: Saye keeps the site active but contained. Ross seals the east entrance, Owen pulls the ground element outside, and Saye uses the thermal profile to prevent a blind re-entry.
operation_plans.yield: No one re-enters Bay 3 blind. The anomaly remains active, but the squad keeps custody of the perimeter and Saye gets enough data to plan the next intervention.
operation_plans.risk: Remote containment leaves Reiss inside the active site longer. If the profile drifts, the column can fail before a second-entry protocol is ready.

lines: Saye takes over the channel and stops asking for status. She gives positions. Owen pulls the ground element outside the collapse radius. Ross seals the east entrance and moves the first responders back another block. Reiss remains at the column, visible through the dust and the emergency lights. The crack keeps moving, but slower. Saye says the thermal profile is stable enough for a second-entry protocol if nobody improvises. Nobody argues with her.

Saye holds the profile long enough to get the squad out, but not long enough to save the evidence bench. The protocol guide is still inside. The wig remains are still on Reiss. The outer cordon is clean and nobody else is hurt. That is what Saye can give them. She files the site as active containment, not resolution. The second-entry plan starts before the dust has settled.

Saye arrives on site and the evidence is already compromised—the protocol guide is missing from the bench, removed by a civilian first responder who thought it was personal property before Ross could stop them. The wig remains are in the evidence container but the chain of custody has a gap. Saye files what she has. The manufacturer's identity is in the report but the physical evidence is incomplete. Reiss is in Institute medical and has not been told anything. Saye sits in the vehicle for a long time after filing. Ross finds her there an hour later. He does not ask what she is thinking. He already knows.

operation_plans.label: Owen: Pull Cordon
operation_plans.tactical_step: failed-entry procedure: pull all personnel from Bay 3, establish outer collapse cordon, preserve life before evidence, and leave the active sign inside until command authorizes re-entry
operation_plans.intent: Owen calls the first entry failed. She pulls Brooks, Park, Torah, and Ross outside the collapse radius and turns the mission from closure to casualty prevention.
operation_plans.yield: The squad survives the failed entry and the cordon holds. Evidence is incomplete and Reiss remains inside, but no one is lost to a preventable second attempt.
operation_plans.risk: Pulling the cordon concedes the room. If Owen loses timing or authority, the evacuation fragments and the squad carries both injury and closure debt into the next packet.

lines: Owen calls it. No second argument, no heroic retry. Brooks is pulled back through the breach point. Park has to be told twice to leave the thermal cutter. Ross moves the civilian responders before they understand why. The ceiling over Bay 3 groans once and then holds. Reiss is still inside. The wig is still active. The squad is outside, alive, and Owen writes the operation status as failed entry, cordon maintained.

Owen gets everyone out, but the cordon is ugly. Park comes through last and hard enough to tear her glove. Brooks is on one knee at the breach point before Ross gets an arm under her. The evidence bench is lost inside the dust. The building holds. The case does not close. Owen does not call it luck. She calls it survival and keeps moving.

Torah speaks the release and the container gets hot. Not warm—hot, the kind of heat that makes Park step back and Owen call everyone to the breach point. The discharge pulse moves through the floor. The temporary column props hold but one of them buckles. The building alarm triggers. Everyone exits. The container is left inside—too hot to carry. Ross seals the east entrance. The instrument readings spike and then drop to a level that Saye, on comms, describes as 'not baseline.' She does not elaborate. The building is condemned. The container is inside it. Torah stands at the cordon line and does not speak for the rest of the operation. Owen files the after-action report. She leaves Torah's role in the resolution section blank and sends it to Saye to complete.

Saye takes over the channel and stops asking for status. She gives positions. Owen pulls the ground element outside the collapse radius. Ross seals the east entrance and moves the first responders back another block. Reiss remains at the column, visible through the dust and the emergency lights. The crack keeps moving, but slower. Saye says the thermal profile is stable enough for a second-entry protocol if nobody improvises. Nobody argues with her.

Owen calls it. No second argument, no heroic retry. Brooks is pulled back through the breach point. Park has to be told twice to leave the thermal cutter. Ross moves the civilian responders before they understand why. The ceiling over Bay 3 groans once and then holds. Reiss is still inside. The wig is still active. The squad is outside, alive, and Owen writes the operation status as failed entry, cordon maintained.

cooldown_borrowed_separation_clean.speaker: brooks
cooldown_borrowed_separation_clean.line_1: Brooks is sitting on the vehicle's rear step with her forearm wrapped, watching Ross redirect a news crew away from the cordon. She has not filed her report.
cooldown_borrowed_separation_clean.line_2: Park comes out of the building with the evidence container and sets it in the cargo bay without looking at Brooks. Brooks watches Park do it and does not ask what is in her expression.

lines: Brooks says, to no one in particular: 'He wasn't trying to hurt anyone.' Park does not answer. Ross comes back from the cordon and sits on the bumper next to Brooks. He says: 'Did Reiss know what he bought?' Brooks looks at her wrapped arm. 'He knew what it promised.' Ross nods. He does not ask the follow-up question. Owen is inside the vehicle, writing. She has been writing for forty minutes. She has not come out. Saye's channel has been quiet since the close. Park opens the cargo bay, checks the container seal, closes it again. She does this three times before she stops. Nobody mentions it.

visible_text: Brooks says, to no one in particular: 'He wasn't trying to hurt anyone.' Park does not answer. Ross comes back from the cordon and sits on the bumper next to Brooks. He says: 'Did Reiss know what he bought?' Brooks looks at her wrapped arm. 'He knew what it promised.' Ross nods. He does not ask the follow-up question. Owen is inside the vehicle, writing. She has been writing for forty minutes. She has not come out. Saye's channel has been quiet since the close. Park opens the cargo bay, checks the container seal, closes it again. She does this three times before she stops. Nobody mentions it.
outcomes: If you ask Brooks: she tells you, flatly, that it felt like pushing against something that had already decided. She does not elaborate. She goes back to watching Ross.

If you ask Park: she shows you her tablet. The second signature is there—same thermal profile, different location, timestamp from six days ago. She does not know where it is. She filed it in her personal notes. She has not told Saye yet. She is telling you now.

If you let it go: Owen comes out of the vehicle with her report. She hands it to Torah without comment. The resolution section has one line: 'Anomaly closed. Remainder unresolved. Manufacturer identity filed for follow-up.' She goes back inside.
followup_hooks: The second heat signature in Park's personal notes: a second Covenant Lock unit, sold to an unknown buyer, active six days ago at an unidentified location
The department head who denied the wig remains request: Saye's full disclosure report will reach them; their response is not yet known
Reiss's recovery: he is in Institute medical and has been told something, but not everything; what he does with what he knows is open
The Separation Athletics manufacturer: the Institute has the name; what the Institute does with it, and how fast, is not Torah's decision

cooldown_borrowed_separation_residual.speaker: brooks
cooldown_borrowed_separation_residual.line_1: Brooks sits on the rear step with her forearm wrapped. The evidence container is sealed, but the case tag still reads residual active.
cooldown_borrowed_separation_residual.line_2: Park keeps checking the thermal trace. Saye has not signed the storage transfer. Owen is waiting for Torah to decide what goes in the debrief.

lines: The site is quiet enough to hear traffic beyond the cordon. That makes the container worse: a sealed object on the cargo bay floor, warm at the edges, labeled as evidence and not as closure. Brooks watches it without looking directly at it. Park has the second heat signature open on her tablet. Saye has written three versions of the custody note and sent none of them.

visible_text: The site is quiet enough to hear traffic beyond the cordon. That makes the container worse: a sealed object on the cargo bay floor, warm at the edges, labeled as evidence and not as closure. Brooks watches it without looking directly at it. Park has the second heat signature open on her tablet. Saye has written three versions of the custody note and sent none of them.
choices: Ask Brooks about the failed hold
Ask Park about the residual signature
outcomes: If you ask Brooks: she says the hold failed before she touched Reiss. She felt the decision in the room before she felt the wall. Then she stops talking and lets Ross finish the bandage.
If you ask Park: she shows you the trace. The second signature is not a theory anymore; it is the same profile, weaker, six days old, somewhere the Institute has not found yet.
If you let Saye seal it: she signs the storage transfer and marks the case residual active. The label is accurate. Nobody likes that it is accurate.
followup_hooks: Residual container remains tagged active in evidence storage; future packets can surface storage risk, audit pressure, or a second release attempt
Park's residual trace confirms a second Covenant Lock unit with the same thermal profile at an unidentified location
Saye's custody note records a suppressed-not-closed anomaly, creating command pressure if the container heats again
Brooks named the failed hold; future debriefs can read whether Torah acknowledged that injury or let it harden

cooldown_borrowed_separation_active.speaker: brooks
cooldown_borrowed_separation_active.line_1: The squad is outside the collapse cordon. Bay 3 is still lit from inside, and Reiss is still at the column.
cooldown_borrowed_separation_active.line_2: Owen has one hand on the radio and one hand on the site map. Saye is building a second-entry protocol. Brooks has not taken her eyes off the breach point.

lines: No one calls it a retreat while Owen can hear them. The civilian responders are two blocks back. Ross is at the outer tape, repeating the same instruction until people stop asking for exceptions. Park is sitting on the curb with the thermal cutter still in her lap. Saye speaks only in measurements. The building has not fallen. The mission has not closed.

visible_text: No one calls it a retreat while Owen can hear them. The civilian responders are two blocks back. Ross is at the outer tape, repeating the same instruction until people stop asking for exceptions. Park is sitting on the curb with the thermal cutter still in her lap. Saye speaks only in measurements. The building has not fallen. The mission has not closed.
choices: Check on Brooks at the cordon
Ask Saye what is still active
outcomes: If you check on Brooks: she says she can go back in. She is wrong, and she knows she is wrong, which is why she does not say it twice.
If you ask Saye: she says the hair is still the load path and the profile is learning from interruption. Then she apologizes for the word learning and does not replace it.
If you hold the cordon: Owen nods once and updates the report to active containment. The word failure appears nowhere in the line she sends to command. It is still in the room with everyone else.
followup_hooks: Bay 3 remains under active containment; future packets can return to the site as a second-entry mission
Saye flagged the load path as adaptive after interruption, raising risk for any future attempt to cut or burn the hair
Brooks wanted back inside despite injury; future command scenes can test whether Owen trusts her judgment under failure conditions
Owen's report avoids the word failure, leaving a review hook if command compares the report to instrument logs

babel_resolution_terminal_partial.speaker: SITREP
babel_resolution_terminal_partial.line_1: Ross has two routing leads cut. The loop has slowed, not stopped, and his shoulder is pinned inside the counter masonry.
babel_resolution_terminal_partial.line_2: Owen has a narrow stairwell window and one moving civilian group. The final routing lead has to be cut or everyone moves under a live terminal.

lines: Ross talks Torah through the last lead without moving his pinned shoulder. Torah reaches into the junction box, finds the lead by touch, and cuts where Ross tells him. The terminal speaker breaks into static and stops. Ross stays pinned until Owen clears the counter edge with the bar. He does not look at his arm until the civilians are moving.

Owen chooses movement over completion. She gets the second-floor group through the stairwell gap on hand signals while Ross stays pinned at the counter and the loop cycles overhead at half speed. The building keeps building, slower now, but every new brick sounds like a clock. Saye marks the terminal as live containment, not closure.

full_disclosure.queued_line: The final lead is cut. The loop is silent, but the authority scatter still has to be documented.

intercept.queued_line: The civilians are moving, but the terminal was not closed cleanly.

babel_resolution_terminal_failed.speaker: SITREP
babel_resolution_terminal_failed.line_1: Ross is down behind the dispatch counter. The routing board is intact. The terminal keeps speaking every ninety seconds.
babel_resolution_terminal_failed.line_2: A new wall segment is rising at the stairwell base. Saye has fourteen minutes on the hardline clock before the roof exit seals.

lines: Owen goes over the counter instead of around it. She gets one hand under Ross and one hand on the brick lip and pulls until the masonry gives a wet crack. Ross comes free with his tablet broken and his arm held tight against his chest. The terminal is still live. Owen points to the roof access with the bar. Nobody argues.

Saye pushes fragmented noise through the hardline before Torah can answer. The terminal stutters. Three wall segments stop mid-rise. The fourth does not. The roof clock pauses but the lobby fills with static so dense Torah can feel it in his teeth. Saye says, "I can hold it. I cannot close it from here." Ross is still behind the counter.

intercept.queued_line: Ross is out, but the hub remains active behind them.

full_disclosure.queued_line: Saye holds the loop from outside, and that is not the same as closing it.

babel_resolution_stairwell_partial.speaker: SITREP
babel_resolution_stairwell_partial.line_1: Owen has broken the scaffold but left a low gap, not an open stairwell. Civilians can crawl through if the squad keeps them calm.
babel_resolution_stairwell_partial.line_2: The terminal is still cycling. Ross can reach the junction box, but a second pole is extending from the opposite wall.

lines: Ross uses the low gap Owen bought him as cover, crosses under the second pole before it sets, and reaches the junction box from the stairwell side. He cuts the routing board while lying on his back in mortar dust. The terminal dies. Owen starts moving civilians through the gap before the silence can become a question.

Torah gets down on one knee and shows the first civilian group how low the gap is with his own body. No command phrase, no radio, no cadence. Just a hand on the floor and a plain instruction: one at a time. They move. The terminal keeps speaking above them, but the stairwell does not close before the last civilian clears it.

full_disclosure.queued_line: Ross kills the board from the stairwell side. The silence still has to be made local before the net comes back.

torah_speaks.queued_line: The civilians are out through the gap, but the dispatch hub is still live.

babel_resolution_stairwell_failed.speaker: SITREP
babel_resolution_stairwell_failed.line_1: The stairwell has set solid. Owen cannot break it from this side. The roof access is the last known exit route.
babel_resolution_stairwell_failed.line_2: The terminal is still broadcasting, and each cycle is adding weight between the squad and the civilians below platform level.

lines: Ross goes back to the counter because the roof route will not matter if the terminal seals it before they arrive. He cuts through the junction housing with shaking hands and finds the board on the second try. The speaker dies. The stairwell remains a wall. Owen is already writing arrows to the roof on scraps of paper and handing them to Torah.

Owen abandons the stairwell. She writes ROOF on her notepad, shows it to Torah, then starts moving. Ross marks the blocked stairwell on his tablet with one hand and keeps the other hand off the radio. The terminal is still alive behind them. Saye says thirteen minutes, then twelve. Owen does not answer. She is counting doors.

full_disclosure.queued_line: The terminal is dead, but the stairwell is a wall and the roof route has become the evacuation plan.

intercept.queued_line: Owen chooses the roof route under a live terminal clock.

babel_cooldown_live_terminal.speaker: agent_caleb_ross
babel_cooldown_live_terminal.line_1: The hub did not close cleanly. The civilians who made it out are coughing mortar dust into paper towels while Ross keeps replaying the terminal diagram on his cracked tablet.
babel_cooldown_live_terminal.line_2: Saye keeps the hardline open after command tells her to clear it. Owen writes every remaining order by hand. Nobody uses the standard net until Torah says the building is behind them.

lines: Ross marks the hub active-containment, not resolved. Saye does not correct him. Owen folds the hand-written route sheet and puts it in her kit instead of the evidence bag.

visible_text: The hub did not close cleanly. The civilians who made it out are coughing mortar dust into paper towels while Ross keeps replaying the terminal diagram on his cracked tablet.
Saye keeps the hardline open after command tells her to clear it. Owen writes every remaining order by hand. Nobody uses the standard net until Torah says the building is behind them.
Ross marks the hub active-containment, not resolved. Saye does not correct him. Owen folds the hand-written route sheet and puts it in her kit instead of the evidence bag.
outcomes: Ross treats the failed closure as a live technical debt instead of a completed mission
Saye accepts command scrutiny to keep the loop from finding a clean channel
Owen avoids the standard net and preserves civilian movement with local paper orders
followup_hooks: The dispatch hub remains a live containment site until the routing board is physically verified
Ross may become fixated on terminal diagrams and access routes in later missions
Owen prefers paper orders after seeing a correct radio protocol build walls

babel_cooldown_roof_exit.speaker: lt_mara_owen
babel_cooldown_roof_exit.line_1: The terminal is dead, but the stairwell is still a wall. The evacuation report calls the roof route successful because everyone who reached it survived.
babel_cooldown_roof_exit.line_2: Owen writes the word successful, crosses it out, and writes passable above it. Ross does not ask why.

lines: Saye files the hub as mechanically silent and structurally hostile. Torah keeps one authority card. Owen keeps the roof map.

visible_text: The terminal is dead, but the stairwell is still a wall. The evacuation report calls the roof route successful because everyone who reached it survived.
Owen writes the word successful, crosses it out, and writes passable above it. Ross does not ask why.
Saye files the hub as mechanically silent and structurally hostile. Torah keeps one authority card. Owen keeps the roof map.
choices: Let Owen amend the report language
outcomes: The mission is recorded as survivable but not clean
Owen carries the distinction between success and passability into future tactical calls
Torah retains a physical reminder that local authority mattered more than approved syntax
followup_hooks: A mechanically silent Babel site can still be structurally hostile
Owen may challenge future reports that call survival a clean success
The kept authority card can return as an artifact or command-state token
